post.content:
An online shopping website has a widget displaying the most popular items bought. 
There is API service that sends a message contains (customer ID, item ID, timestamp) after each purchase transaction. Find the most popular items to be displayed on the widget.

I would like to hear your opinions, because I proposed two different solutions, and I didn't pass though.
Note: It's a live coding problem though, they wanted me to write a code to show the top K items.

**Some ideas/hints worth to mention:**
* the top K items should updated every N minutes (let's say 10 mins), because it's a widget, and it should change with time.
* The API service sends million of logs every minute, so the the required code should be flexible, and doesn't save everything in the memory first.
* 
Thank you.

Hello OP,

Seems like a Top K Frequent problem and interviewer was hinting you towards updating the heap every 10 minutes: https://leetcode.com/problems/top-k-frequent-elements/solutions/81635/3-java-solution-using-array-maxheap-treemap/comments/158484
The Bucket Sort approach would not work here as it will have huge memory implications(saving all the products and their purchase frequency in memory, which could have been costly).
Good luck for future.

This sounds more like a Design Interview than a Coding Interview. Anyways, in terms of a coding Interview, I think you\'d have a Map<int, int> -> (item ID, count), and then possibly run a QuicksSelect on it every 10 minutes to find the Top-K so that\'s Time and Space efficient. I\'m not sure it\'s possible to do better for a Coding interview.

These could be the algorithm:

Algorithm maintains 
-> a hash map to count the frequency of items.
-> an array of size K

Now for a message coming from an API service, O(N)
1. Update the frequency of items in the hash map. O(1)
2. Add the latest item to the array by its frequency using the insertion sort O(N)

Now 2nd can be O(log N) if it is a well-balanced binary tree.

Interesting that you got basically a SDI question as your phone screen. Neat.

From studying for SDI interviews this sounds like a thinly veiled ["design top K"](https://www.hellointerview.com/learn/system-design/answer-keys/top-k) where in your case `k=1` maybe.

Some common considerations:
* the value of `k`: literally just the one top element? Or maybe top 5 in case user already clicked "stop showing me this" on the top three?
* time windows: the most purchased product ever? Past month/week/day/hour?
* other weights: top `k` is basically a scoring question; are there other parts of score than just number of sales? Maybe we want to highly products that are on sale or sponsored

Common design patterns:
* async receiving clicks versus maintaining top `k`
  * ultimately *some* service (even if "only" a mere message broker like Kafka/Redis/RabbitMQ) needs to respond synchronously or near-synchronously to the (customId, itemId, timestamp)
  * but you can afford some latency when updating popular products, they usually don't change faster than every 5-10 minutes
  * so usually a message queue shows up to act as a middleman/buffer between a microservice that pushes transaction events (the triples) and another microservice that polls for updates so it can maintain a top-K structure. Buffering events reduces pressure on the top-K solution (see next point), it lets you have a Combiner in MapReduce lingo
* various solutions for maintaining the top K
  * the Hello Interview link suggests rolling your own in-memory sharded top-K service
    * pro: the most efficient and general, you write it so you get to do whatever
    * con: high development and deployment cost to deploy your own parallel microservice
  * my favored approach: sharded SQL and slap a tree index on the score column
    * pro: SQL engines have spent a LOT of time getting trees and query performance fast, so why reinvent the wheel?
    * con: less flexible since you buy into SQL
* caching
  * regardless of the backend for getting top-K, the actual top-K doesn't change over that fast. Even the top product of the last hour can be updated on a 5 minute delay if necessary -> a TTL of 10% of the window or so seems reasonable
  * for maximum caching performance you can make queries about the top-K rest queries whose headers tell clients and ISPs to cache the result until a specific expiration time so clients' browsers serve as the first line of defense
  * and the usual "let's just store a bunch of stuff in Redis" works on the server side
* API structure
  * the Big Four are REST, GraphQL, websocket, SSE (server-sent events), but always keep the fifth wheel of "custom protocol" like protobuf/avro in your back pocket
  * in this case you'd almost certainly pick REST
